problem_summary: 2025 MCM/ICM Problem B (discrete) — sustainable tourism for Juneau, Alaska. The team had to build a tourism model that balances cruise-tourism revenue against overtourism harms (Mendenhall Glacier retreat, strained water/waste infrastructure, resident discontent), plan how to spend extra tax/fee revenue, run sensitivity analysis to find key drivers, transfer the model to other destinations, and write a one-page memo to Juneau's tourism committee. This paper (team 2505199) frames it as a three-objective optimization: maximize tourism revenue (Z1), minimize ecological/environmental stress (Z3), and maximize combined resident+tourist satisfaction (Z2), with 8 government-controlled decision variables.
models_used: Multi-objective optimization model with 3 conflicting objectives (Z1 revenue max, Z2 satisfaction max, Z3 environmental-stress min) over 8 decision variables: tourist count x, daily limit y1, alcohol-sales restriction y2, tourism tax y3, tourist fee y4, and three spend allocations y5 environmental / y6 infrastructure / y7 promotion

NSGA-II (Non-dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm II, Deb et al. 2002) as the solver — non-dominated sorting into Pareto fronts, crowding-distance for diversity, tournament selection, Simulated Binary Crossover (SBX), and polynomial mutation
Gradient descent with MSE loss to fit the linear/exponential sub-models (e.g. transportation revenue D = lambda2*x + eps2) and estimate the ~45 lambda coefficients

Exponential saturation/asymptote functions to model diminishing returns — alcohol-restriction impact on catering C = lambda1*x - lambda5*exp(-y2/lambda5), and the base-tourist saturation curve A = lambda29*(1 - exp(-y1/lambda29))

Normalized weighted-sum sub-aggregation inside each objective (e.g. Z3 weights omega3/omega4/omega5 over carbon M, water J, waste K, each divided by its max; Z2 weights resident vs tourist satisfaction with omega1+omega2=1)
Pareto-front visualization (3D surface over economy/community/environment) for trade-off-based selection of a single recommended operating point
Single-parameter and two-parameter (heatmap) sensitivity analysis sweeping y1..y7
key_techniques: Decomposing revenue into named additive components (catering C, transport D, accommodation E, shopping F, plus policy-driven 'other attractions' income R) and grounding each component's share with a real pie chart (catering 28%, accommodation 33%, etc.)

Final profit defined as Z1 = O + P - Q: main revenue O + additional income P (fees y4*x + tax y3*O) minus hidden costs Q (infrastructure I, carbon-footprint cost L, management H, promotion y7) — directly answering the prompt's 'hidden costs' and 'extra-income spending plan' asks

Closing the loop from tourism back to the glacier: carbon emissions M -> glacier retreat rate N = M*lambda35 -> hard constraint N <= W, making the environmental objective causally tied to economic activity rather than a bolt-on penalty
Min-max normalization of every heterogeneous term (dollars, carbon tonnes, congestion index) before weighting, so objectives at vastly different scales combine sensibly
Modeling diminishing returns explicitly with exponentials so the optimizer cannot game unbounded linear gains (raising the daily limit y1 stops adding tourists past saturation)

Rich constraint taxonomy: tourist-number caps (x <= 365*y1 and x <= 365*(A+B)), glacier protection, resource carrying capacity (water/waste/energy ceilings lambda36-38), minimum-level floors (satisfaction, env spend), and non-negativity

Generalizing rather than re-deriving for Part 2: each new city only edits the relevant module — Hawaii adds a seawater-pollution term X to Z3 and drops the glacier constraint; Yosemite raises infrastructure coefficients lambda11/lambda42; New Orleans adds a cultural-damage index Y with a cap; New York raises the promotion coefficient lambda12
why_it_won: From a judge's view this paper wins on three things. (1) It chose a method that fits the problem: overtourism is inherently a trade-off among economy, ecology, and society, and a 3-objective Pareto formulation with NSGA-II represents that honestly instead of collapsing it into one weighted score — the Pareto front itself becomes the argument. (2) It delivers a concrete, actionable answer the committee can read off directly (12,000 tourists/day, 8% tax, $15 fee, 30/40/20 spend split), not just a method. (3) Completeness and prompt-fidelity: it explicitly addresses hidden costs, the extra-income allocation plan, sensitivity-driven key-factor identification, a genuinely modular four-city transfer, and a clean one-page memo — every sub-task of Problem B is visibly checked off. The causal glacier→carbon→constraint link and the data-anchored revenue breakdown signal real domain engagement rather than a generic template.
section_highlights: Strong structure throughout. The 'Our Work' flowchart (Figure 2) maps the entire pipeline up front. Notation Table 1 declares every symbol before use, and the model-building section narrates the rationale for each equation (why exponential for alcohol impact, why linear for transport) instead of dumping formulas. The NSGA-II section (4.2) is textbook-clean: it walks population init -> fitness -> non-dominated sorting -> crowding distance -> SBX crossover -> polynomial mutation -> elitist update, with the dominance definition stated explicitly. Results (4.3) tie the chosen operating point back to the Pareto surface to argue rationality. Section 7 (regional promotion) is the standout for impact — four cities, each with a specific equation/coefficient edit, showing genuine generalizability. The memo is correctly formatted (From/To/Date/Subject) and reader-appropriate.
pitfalls_or_limitations: The ~45 lambda coefficients are presented as 'fitted by gradient descent' but the underlying data is never shown, sourced, or cited; many values (e.g. lambda1=1257.94, glacier coefficient lambda35=1e-6) appear assumed/hand-tuned, so the precise outputs (12,000, 8%, $15) are not really verifiable — a sharp judge would discount the exactness

Internal inconsistency between body and memo: the body says 8% tax / 30-40-20 spend split, while the memo states an '$80-per-person tourism tax' and a 30/40/30 split (which sums to 100 but contradicts the body's 20% promotion) — a proofreading failure that undercuts credibility

Many figures are placeholder or broken: the 3D Pareto 'surface' data is a degenerate diagonal (all axes equal 0.2,0.4,...), the third sensitivity chart is entirely empty, and the multi-parameter heatmap table is garbled — the visuals don't actually substantiate the claims they caption

Heavy reliance on linearity and stability assumptions (fixed per-capita spend, fixed carrying capacity, linear resident-satisfaction response); the paper acknowledges this in Weaknesses but it limits real-world validity
Glacier retreat reduced to a single linear proportionality N = M*lambda35 with lambda35 = 1e-6 — physically thin for what is the problem's signature environmental harm

NSGA-II hyperparameters (population size N0, generations, crossover/mutation probabilities, distribution indices eta_c/eta_m) are described generically but never reported, so the run is not reproducible

Demand is treated as something the optimizer sets via x rather than something that responds endogenously to price (tax/fee) and crowding — the feedback from policy to actual demand is only partially captured in the B fluctuation term
reusable_patterns: For any 'balance competing goals' prompt, formulate as multi-objective + NSGA-II and present the Pareto front as the core deliverable, then justify a single recommended point against it — avoids the arbitrary-weights trap of a single weighted sum

Build the objective bottom-up from named, individually-justified sub-terms (revenue = catering + transport + ... ; cost = water + waste + carbon + ...) so the model reads as auditable accounting, and anchor component weights with a real data chart
Use the profit identity 'final = main + additional - hidden costs' to cleanly absorb prompt asks about externalities and extra-revenue spending into the objective itself
Always min-max normalize heterogeneous terms before combining; state the weights and that they sum to 1
Encode diminishing returns with exponential/saturation curves so the optimizer can't exploit unbounded linear terms
Make the environmental objective causal: trace activity -> emissions -> physical harm -> hard constraint, rather than adding an unexplained penalty

For the 'apply to other places' sub-question, keep the core model fixed and demonstrate modularity — each new context edits exactly one module (add a variable, change one coefficient, drop one constraint); this is far more convincing than re-deriving
Open with a one-figure 'Our Work' pipeline flowchart and a complete notation table; close with a correctly-formatted, audience-appropriate memo
Run both single-parameter and pairwise (heatmap) sensitivity sweeps to identify dominant drivers and interactions — but ensure the figures actually render and match the text
